title: Bar Chart: The spending (in millions) by 18-20 year olds in one country on five different pro...
description: The chart below shows the spending (in millions) by 18-20 year olds in one country on five different products in 2010 and 2014. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
guide_html:
HƯỚNG DẪN VIẾT BÀI

IELTS Writing Task 1 – Spending by young people (2010 vs 2014)

1. Hiểu đề bài

Biểu đồ thể hiện mức chi tiêu (triệu đô) của nhóm 18–20 tuổi cho 5 mặt hàng:

  • Food
  • Clothes
  • Books
  • Smartphones
  • Toiletries

trong hai năm 2010 và 2014.

Dạng này yêu cầu so sánh theo thời gian và giữa các nhóm sản phẩm.

2. Introduction

Paraphrase đề, giữ các ý chính:

  • spending
  • young people
  • five products
  • two years

3. Overview

  • Food là khoản chi cao nhất trong cả hai năm.
  • Books là thấp nhất và giảm đáng kể.
  • Phần lớn các mặt hàng tăng vào năm 2014, đặc biệt là smartphones.

4. Cách chia Body

BODY 1 – Nhóm chi tiêu cao

Food:

3700 → 4300 (tăng rõ rệt, luôn đứng đầu)

Smartphones:

2000 → 2700 (tăng mạnh, nổi bật nhất)

BODY 2 – Nhóm còn lại

Clothes:

2500 → 2450 (giảm nhẹ)

Books:

1000 → 500 (giảm mạnh nhất)

Toiletries:

1900 → 2000 (tăng nhẹ)

5. Gợi ý diễn đạt

  • spending → expenditure
  • increased → rose
  • decreased → fell
  • slightly → marginally

Câu chốt

Food cao nhất, smartphones tăng mạnh, books giảm mạnh nhất.

The bar chart compares spending by 18–20-year-olds on five product categories in 2010 and 2014.

Overall, food and clothes accounted for the largest shares of expenditure in both years, while books received the least. Spending increased in most categories over time, with the notable exception of books, which saw a sharp decline.

In 2010, young people spent the most on food at 3,700 million, followed by clothes at 2,500 million. Smartphones and toiletries accounted for 2,000 million and 1,900 million, respectively, while books were the least purchased category at 1,000 million.

By 2014, expenditure on food rose significantly to 4,300 million, remaining the highest figure. Spending on smartphones also increased markedly, from 2,000 million to 2,700 million, making it the third-largest category. Toiletries saw a slight rise to 2,000 million, while clothes decreased marginally to 2,450 million.

In contrast, spending on books dropped considerably to just 500 million, representing the most substantial change across all categories. Overall, the data suggests a shift in priorities, with greater spending on technology and essentials, and reduced interest in books.
